summaries.summary:
**Key Decisions**
- Banks failing to conduct credit awareness and MSME camps (ICICI, APSCAB, CBI, Yes Bank, Bandhan Bank, IndusInd, UCO, Union Bank, IOB, BOM, Axis Bank, APRB) must organize camps by April 2021
- Planning Department to remove 10-loan cap per district under Deen Dayal Swavalamban Yojana and include APRB as eligible lender
- Government threatened penalties including withdrawal of deposits from non-performing banks, particularly those not implementing PMEGP and SHG lending schemes

**Performance Highlights**
- CD ratio remains critically low across the state, drawing sharp criticism from government officials
- Street vendor lending saw 2,017 sanctions but 1,523 disbursals with 1,120 rejections due to beneficiaries being untraceable/non-genuine
- Financial inclusion progress: 1,877 out of 1,927 unbanked villages covered, leaving 50 villages still unbanked (43 allotted to APSCAB, 6 to ICICI, 1 to BOB)
- Only SBI and PNB sanctioned loans under PMAY scheme (41 accounts worth ₹5.07 crores)

**Action Items**
- APSCAB, BOB, and ICICI to cover remaining 50 unbanked villages by April 2021 using cluster approach
- All banks to submit NPA account lists to district administrations and utilize Lok Adalats for recovery
- Indian Bank to open branch at Seppa by September 2021; Canara Bank to open branch at Chayangtajo

**Key Decisions**
• Finance Department to withdraw government deposits from INDUSIND, BANDHAN BANK & YES BANK due to CD ratios below 20%
• HDFC, PSB, CBI, BOM, ICICI, BOB and NESFB placed on caution list for CD ratios below 30%, required to improve by June 2025
• New FY 2025-26 targets approved for social security schemes (PMJJBY, PMSBY, APY) across all districts and banks
• Banks scoring less than 20% on performance matrix must submit action plans

**Performance Highlights**
• Deposits grew 12.38% YOY and advances increased 16.36% YOY, improving CD ratio from 38.60% to 39.97%
• Overall ACP target achieved for FY 2024-25, but Agriculture sector only reached 83.56% and Other Priority sector 51.24%
• 4 new bank branches opened during the year in Seppa, Tezu, Roing, and Basar

**Action Items**
• All banks to dispose of remaining 37 pending DDUSY loan applications by June 20, 2025
• PNB to explain significant drop in Housing Loan portfolio from Rs. 54.64 crores to Rs. 2.29 crores at next SLBC meeting
• Banks to submit inoperative accounts and unclaimed deposit data to SLBC by September 20, 2025

**Key Decisions**
- Finance Department directed to withdraw government deposits from INDUSIND, BANDHAN BANK & YES BANK due to CD ratios below 20%
- Seven banks (HDFC, PSB, CBI, BOM, ICICI, BOB, NESFB) placed on caution list for CD ratios below 30%, must improve by June 2025
- All pending DDUSY loan applications (37 remaining) must be disposed of by June 20, 2025
- New branch opening targets set: 5 branches across districts including Lemmi (PNB), Tali (APRB), and Sangram (SBI) by September 2025

**Performance Highlights**
- Strong financial growth with deposits up 12.38% YoY and advances up 16.36% YoY, improving CD ratio from 38.60% to 39.97%
- Overall ACP target achieved for FY 2024-25, but sector-wise shortfalls in Agriculture (83.56% achievement) and Other Priority sectors (51.24%)
- PNB reported unexplained drop in Housing Loans from ₹54.64 crores to ₹2.29 crores, causing ₹54.17 crore degrowth in Other Priority sector

**Action Items**
- Banks must submit inoperative accounts and unclaimed deposit data to SLBC by specific deadlines
- ATMA NIRBHAR loan proposals to be completed on priority with monthly reporting by LDMs to district officials
- Enhanced publicity campaigns required for social security schemes (PMJJBY, PMSBY, APY) to meet FY 2025-26 targets

**Key Decisions**
- Government of Arunachal Pradesh approved the SLBC meeting minutes for Q4 2024 (quarter ended December 2024)
- Corrections were mandated for three senior officer names in the minutes: Commissioner Agriculture (Shri Bidol Tayeng, IAS), Secretary Horticulture (Smti Koj Rinya, IFS), and Secretary Industries (Miss Bullo Mamu, IRS)
- Instructions issued to finalize and distribute corrected minutes to all government departments, banks, and stakeholders

**Performance Highlights**
- SLBC meeting was successfully conducted on February 12, 2025, covering Q4 2024 performance
- Meeting involved participation from key government departments including Agriculture, Horticulture, and Industries

**Action Items**
- State Bank of India (Lead Bank Office) to issue corrected minutes with proper officer names to all concerned parties
- All stakeholders including government departments and banks to initiate appropriate follow-up actions based on the approved minutes
- Distribution to be completed to ensure implementation of meeting decisions across the banking network

*Note: This document appears to be an administrative approval letter rather than the actual meeting minutes, so specific banking performance data and detailed decisions from the SLBC meeting are not available in this excerpt.*

**Key Decisions**
- Government of Arunachal Pradesh approved SLBC meeting minutes for quarters ended June 2024 and September 2024 (meeting held on November 13, 2024)
- Future SLBC minutes must be submitted for approval within one week of the meeting
- SLBC meeting for quarter ended December 2024 is now due and date scheduling letter to be submitted

**Performance Highlights**
- The approval process experienced delays, with minutes submitted over two months after the November 2024 meeting
- Administrative correction needed for Dr. Sonal Swaroop's name spelling in official records

**Action Items**
- State Bank of India (Lead Bank Office) to finalize and distribute approved minutes to all concerned parties with intimation to Finance Department
- Submit letter to fix date for next SLBC meeting covering December 2024 quarter
- Correct the spelling of Dr. Sonal Swaroop's name in future documentation

**Key Decisions**
- Banks required to complete coverage of all villages with population 1000-1999 by March 31, 2013 as part of financial inclusion strategy
- Low-performing banks (Union Bank 15% CD ratio, Central Bank 24%, Indian Bank 25%) directed to increase lending to improve state-wide credit-deposit ratio
- RSETIs (Rural Self Employment Training Institutes) expansion prioritized with banks and government to accelerate opening of remaining 12 out of 27 allocated centers

**Performance Highlights**
- Strong annual growth: advances up 28.41% and deposits up 14.05% year-over-year, with CD ratio improving from 44.13% to 45.06%
- ACP (Annual Credit Plan) achievement reached 17.59% in Q1 against ₹6420 crore target, with services sector leading at 30% achievement
- Financial inclusion lagging: only 297 out of 5224 allocated villages covered, and 590,631 out of 1,185,456 families provided bank accounts

**Action Items**
- Banks to expedite Kisan Credit Card issuance with government support for online land record updates to accelerate processing
- LDMs in districts with low per capita credit (Morigaon, Darrang, Karbi Anglong, Barpeta, Hailakandi, Karimganj) to coordinate with Deputy Commissioners for credit enhancement
- Monthly progress reports required for Weavers Credit Card targets and MSME lending recovery after negative 0.94% growth in Q1

**Key Decisions**
- SLBC approved renewal of KCCs from Rs.50,000 to Rs.1,00,000 based on Land Cultivation Certificates issued by Village Level Extension Workers and countersigned by Agricultural Extension Officers
- Banks with CD ratio below 20% were directed to take effective steps to increase their ratios (6 banks identified including HSBC, Kotak Mahindra, Vijaya Bank, BOM, P&SB, and ANB)
- Union Bank of India and Assam Gramin Vikash Bank were specifically assigned to cover the remaining 2 villages with population above 5000

**Performance Highlights**
- Credit growth showed positive momentum with advances increasing by Rs.908 crores (16.38% YoY) and deposits by Rs.704 crores (12.03% YoY)
- Annual Credit Plan achievement was concerning at only 43% (Rs.4,539 crores against Rs.10,670 crores target), with Agriculture at 43%, Industry at 27%, and Services at 67%
- Financial inclusion progress was moderate with 3,380,780 households covered out of 6,367,295 total households (approximately 50%)

**Action Items**
- Banks must accelerate household coverage to complete financial inclusion targets, particularly covering remaining households in service area villages
- Union Bank of India directed to take action regarding non-submission of data by LDM Tinsukia and ensure timely SLBC data uploading
- Government of Assam to expedite land allotment for remaining RSETIs (11 out of 27 target still pending land allocation)

**Key Decisions**
- Credit flow to agriculture needs to increase beyond the 19.31% achieved (against 18% benchmark), given that 85% of Assam's population depends on agriculture
- Banks must establish better outreach programs and connect with farmers through Business Correspondent channels and village-level officials
- PMJDY household survey deadline extended to October 31, 2014 due to natural calamities (originally September 30, 2014)
- Banks directed to restructure agricultural loans in flood-affected areas, particularly Goalpara and Dhemaji districts

**Performance Highlights**
- Agricultural advances reached Rs. 7,219 crores (19.31% of total bank credit), comprising Rs. 3,184 crores in crop loans and Rs. 4,035 crores in term loans
- KCC performance extremely poor: only 33,753 loans achieved against annual target of 764,500 in first quarter
- Under PMJDY, 1,390,445 accounts opened covering 2,204 out of 3,699 SSAs
- Credit-Deposit ratio remained low at 43.97%

**Action Items**
- Banks to link PMJDY accounts with Kisan Credit Cards to improve agricultural credit penetration
- Establish regular block-level and district-level consultative committee meetings for agricultural growth planning
- Complete PMJDY household survey by October 31, 2014, and improve data collection systems for timely reporting

**Key Decisions**
- All banks directed to cover remaining households under financial inclusion by March 31, 2015, ahead of the original March 2016 deadline
- Quarterly targets to be set for all schemes for better monitoring at SLBC meetings
- Union Bank of India committed to open Brick and Mortar branch at Chaisimana NC within next quarter
- Banks mandated to conduct one Financial Literacy Camp per month in rural branches, with focus on one block per district monthly

**Performance Highlights**
- PMJDY achieved significant coverage with 29.05 lakh accounts opened and Rs.173 crores deposited across all districts
- CD ratio improved from 42.79% in March 2015 to 48.88% in June 2015
- ACP achievement increased from Rs.1024 crores in June 2014 to Rs.1952 crores in June 2015
- KCC performance disappointing at only 50% target achievement due to branches demanding collateral security despite Rs.1 lakh limit enhancement

**Action Items**
- RBI to take action against Corporation Bank for non-submission of data and absence from SLBC meetings
- Banks to issue KCCs up to Rs.1 lakh based on Land Cultivation Certificates without collateral security requirements
- All banks to improve Financial Literacy Camp frequency, particularly focusing on rural areas where response has been poor except for AGVB

**Key Decisions**
- All contesting candidates for upcoming Assembly elections must have individual bank accounts with all expenditure routed through these accounts via crossed cheques
- Banks directed to improve CD ratio performance (currently 49.88%) with special sub-committees formed for low-performing districts 
- All banks required to upload branch-wise data including WCC, PMEGP, NRLM and MSME loan status on SLBC website
- Special campaigns mandated for Rupay Card activation due to poor performance

**Performance Highlights**  
- Mudra Loans sanctioned totaling Rs.1109.93 crores across Assam by December 2015
- ACP achievement improved to 40% in September 2015-16 versus 24% in previous year
- 22,605 WCC forms remained pending from March 2015, with 5,602 fresh forms sent to banks by November 2015
- Several banks including ICICI, Axis, Dena Bank, OBC showed poor performance in various schemes

**Action Items**
- Banks to submit updated Rupay Card delivery and activation data by January 15, 2016
- Department of Industry to make all relevant formats available on website by April 2016
- SLBC to discuss new Assam Agricultural Land Regulation Act in detail at next meeting as regulations expected by January 2016

**Key Decisions**
- All banks directed to upload complete SLBC data to website (not partially), with Kotak Mahindra and Karnataka Bank specifically required to submit reasons for non-participation
- Sub-committees to be formed immediately in districts with CD ratio below 40%, with progress reports required at next SLBC meeting
- Every bank branch mandated to conduct at least one Financial Literacy Camp (FLC) monthly and upload bank-wise data
- Pre-SLBC meetings to be conducted before each main SLBC meeting to discuss gaps

**Performance Highlights**
- Banks achieved Guinness World Record for account opening under Prime Minister Jan Dhan Yojana scheme
- NABARD programs conducted: LDRB (1,440 in Dima Hasao and Karbi Anglong), AGVB (226), and NABARD (187)
- Higher education loan disbursement in Assam identified as below national average
- Weavers Credit Card scheme lacking popularity, to be replaced by new "Weavers Mudra Scheme"

**Action Items**
- SLBC to report on extension of PMJDY benefits (insurance, pension, Rs. 5,000 overdraft) to all account holders
- Banks and NABARD to develop business models for bamboo farming and transport vehicle financing for Numaligarh Refinery's bamboo-to-ethanol project
- Increase MUDRA loan sanctions to improve CD ratios, with progress tracking as action point for next meeting

**Key Decisions**
- Government allocated Rs. 500 crores for Chief Minister's Samagra Gramya Unnayan Yojana (CMSGUY) targeting all-round village development across 25,425 census villages
- SLBC set Priority Sector lending target of Rs. 11,000 crores for FY 2016-17
- Formation of committee under Sri Dipok Kumar Barthakur to monitor credit flow in the state
- Introduction of computerized Bakijai System for online loan recovery with full government support to banks

**Performance Highlights**
- Best performing banks recognized: Bandhan Bank (highest CD ratio), SBI (leading in TARUN MUDRA loans and Priority Sector advances), HDFC (top in SISHU MUDRA loans)
- Chief Minister expressed concerns about MUDRA scheme implementation, noting some banks reportedly refused PMMY loan sanctions
- State CD ratio remains a concern, prompting focus on rural credit expansion

**Action Items**
- All banks to strengthen implementation of MUDRA and other "flexi schemes" in letter and spirit
- Banks to support organic tea sector development for 1,25,000 small tea growers and provide expertise for youth skill development programs in 25,000 villages
- SLBC Convenor to start future meetings with review and felicitation of best performing banks across various categories

**Key Decisions**
- Banks directed to increase focus on industrial lending to improve Assam's low Credit-Deposit (CD) ratio compared to national average
- Government committed to provide full support for NPA recovery and reducing bad loans
- Financial sector urged to support 200 Primary Processing Centres planned for current year, expanding to 700-800 centres within 2-3 years
- Banks instructed to prioritize Swachh Bharat Mission toilet upgrades, declared as Priority Sector lending by RBI in 2015

**Performance Highlights**
- Poor performance in government schemes: only 1% of 3,000 PMEGP cases sanctioned; SBI sanctioned only two loans under Credit Guarantee Scheme
- Stand-Up India scheme severely underperforming with only 466 proposals sanctioned out of target 4,354, disbursing ₹45 crores
- Growing investor interest evidenced at "Prabashi Bhartiya Diwas" in Bengaluru with long queues at Assam pavilion
- Major industrial commitments secured including 100 acres sanctioned for Baba Ramdev's Food Park, with Dabur and Havel also showing investment interest

**Action Items**
- Banks to focus on MSME sector lending, particularly agro-based processing industries to support state's agrarian economy
- Financial institutions to increase participation in Credit Guarantee Scheme and Stand-Up India for SC/ST and women borrowers
- Government to develop waterways from Sadiya to Dhubri, enhance rail connectivity, and establish world-class Skill Development Centre

**Key Decisions**
- State government announced zero interest crop loans with 100% interest waiver for loans up to Rs. 1 lakh, allocating Rs. 25 crore for the scheme
- SVAYEM youth empowerment scheme launched with Rs. 200 crore budget for 1 lakh beneficiaries, featuring 20% government subsidy and Rs. 100 crore risk fund
- Chief Minister Samagra Gramya Unnayan Yojana approved with Rs. 2500 crore subsidy allocation, targeting 6000 tractors in FY 2017-18 with 70% back-ended subsidy
- Kanaklata Mahila Sabalikaran Yojana for women empowerment allocated Rs. 250 crore budget

**Performance Highlights**
- Credit-Deposit ratio improved from 46.44% (December 2016) to 47.26% (March 2017) despite demonetization impact
- Priority sector advances increased substantially with Annual Credit Plan targets fully achieved
- 8 lakh bank accounts already opened in tea garden areas, earning SLBC the Chief Minister's Award for Excellence

**Action Items**
- Banks must coordinate with Deputy Commissioners and Lead District Managers to submit tea garden worker account data by August 15, 2017 for Rs. 5000 incentive disbursement (Rs. 287 crore allocated)
- KCC renewal incentive of Rs. 3000 per renewal announced with Rs. 65 crore budget allocation to improve performance
- All SLBC member banks requested to sign MoUs with Industries & Commerce Department for CMSGUY scheme implementation

**Key Decisions**
- Each bank branch in Assam must disburse 3 new MUDRA loans on December 6, 2017 (Mahaparinirvan Diwas) to specific categories: one Scheduled Caste beneficiary, one woman, and one Scheduled Tribe beneficiary (or OBC if ST not available)
- Banks required to display banners with beneficiary names in banking halls and submit compliance reports to SLBC by December 8, 2017
- State government announced "Swami Vivekanand Assam Youth Empowerment Yojana" offering Rs. 1 lakh for new businesses and Rs. 2 lakhs for existing businesses with state subsidies

**Performance Highlights**
- Assam successfully implemented demonetization with least public inconvenience, acknowledged by Ministry of Finance
- 6 lakh new accounts opened in tea garden areas during demonetization period
- Under PMJDY: 1.23 crore accounts opened; MUDRA loans: 1.17 lakh loans sanctioned (95,137 Sishu, 18,423 Kishore, 3,807 Tarun)
- Banking infrastructure: 34 banks, 2,400 branches, 6,000 BC/CSPs serving 3.12 crore population

**Action Items**
- State government to provide Rs. 2,500 DBT to tea garden workers' accounts, with additional Rs. 2,500 after six months of account maintenance
- Banks to focus on financial inclusion in unbanked areas with poor internet connectivity
- All banks to coordinate for comprehensive coverage of remaining unbanked areas across the state

**Key Decisions**
- Sub-committees must be formed in districts where Credit-Deposit (CD) ratio is below 60% national benchmark, with mandatory meetings and action plans to be ratified by DLCC and SLBC
- All banks required to issue RuPay cards to account holders and ensure PIN delivery and activation as part of financial inclusion efforts
- Government of Assam to develop a matrix scale system similar to Bihar for distributing government deposits based on banks' priority sector lending performance and rural branch reach

**Performance Highlights**
- MUDRA loan target achievement of 76% (5,373 loans disbursed against 7,047 target) on December 6, 2017, with 10 banks achieving 100% targets including SBI, HDFC Bank, and Canara Bank
- CD ratio improved marginally from 47.26% (June 2017) to 47.81% (September 2017), though still below national benchmark of 60%
- Agriculture advances at 23% of total advances, exceeding RBI benchmark of 18%

**Action Items**
- Lead banks (SBI and UBI) to convene CD ratio sub-committee meetings in underperforming districts as per RBI guidelines
- All banks to provide borrower data to Agriculture Department for database reconciliation and interest subvention verification
- Banks that failed to meet MUDRA loan targets on December 6 to complete remaining disbursements for special report to Governor

**Key Decisions**
- Launched SVAYEM (Swami Vivekananda Assam Youth Empowerment) scheme targeting one lakh unemployed youth with loans up to Rs. 1 lakh for new entrepreneurs and Rs. 2 lakh for existing ones
- Allocated Rs. 300 crores for SVAYEM implementation with 20% government subsidy, 25% beneficiary contribution, and 55% bank financing
- Set application deadline for SVAYEM as May 30, 2018, with district-level committees to screen and recommend proposals to banks
- Established Rs. 100 crores Credit Risk Guarantee Cover for SVAYEM loans up to 55% of NPA dues

**Performance Highlights**  
- CD Ratio improved from 47.81% (September 2017) to 51.77% (December 2017) due to higher advance growth relative to deposits
- Banks nearly achieved Annual Credit Plan targets with expectation of 100% achievement by March 2018
- Strong performance in government schemes PMJDY, PMJJBY, and PMSBY, though concerns raised about low PMEGP progress against 6,000 target

**Action Items**
- All banks directed to cooperate in ongoing Gram Swaraj Abhiyan campaign (April 14 - May 5, 2018) covering 3,000 villages in Assam
- Banks instructed to actively participate in SVAYEM implementation through district committees and provide necessary financing
- Extension officers deployed in each district to coordinate with banks for proper loan recovery under new schemes

**Key Decisions**
- Banks directed to upload all data fields on SLBC portal from June 2018 quarter onwards, with responsibility assigned directly to individual banks
- SLBC meetings must send invitations at least 7 days in advance and include Action Taken Reports from previous meetings
- Government requested SLBC to standardize parameters ensuring banks with lower CD ratios don't dominate government fund allocation

**Performance Highlights**
- Annual Credit Plan achieved 116% of target (Rs. 22,464 crores against Rs. 19,440 crores target) with Priority Sector at 57% of total advances
- SME segment showed exceptional 277% growth, indicating strong employment generation potential
- Gram Swaraj Abhiyan performance disappointing at only 32% achievement, well below expectations

**Action Items**
- All banks must intensify efforts to improve Gram Swaraj Abhiyan performance with government assurance of administrative support
- SLBC Convenor to implement RBI guidelines on Lead Bank Scheme revamping issued April 6, 2018
- Banks with no PMMY portal achievements (Indian Overseas Bank, Punjab National Bank, Syndicate Bank, Corporation Bank) must rectify data reporting immediately

**Key Decisions**
- Established four sub-committees for Priority Sector Lending, Government-sponsored loans, Technical issues, and NPA management to improve coordination and monitoring
- Set February 15, 2019 as deadline for implementation of all government-sponsored programmes for 2018-19
- Mandated all banks to provide nodal officer contact details for MSME sector financing on the state portal
- Required banks to submit standardized KCC loan formats, timeframes, and document requirements by November 30, 2018

**Performance Highlights**
- Credit-Deposit Ratio improved to 52.39% from 50.44% in March 2018, with advances growing by ₹3,734 crores
- Priority Sector Advances reached 58.64% of total advances, with Agriculture segment at 20.87% (above benchmark)
- Achieved 44% of Annual Credit Plan target by second quarter and over 100% performance in Financial Inclusion campaigns (GSA-I and GSA-II)

**Action Items**
- Underperforming banks (Andhra Bank, IDBI, Syndicate Bank, Federal Bank, ICICI Bank) must accelerate growth across sectors
- All banks to extend agricultural loans through SHGs and Joint Liability Groups as alternative delivery mechanism
- MSME Department to conduct district-level workshops for online applications and circulate scheme details to all banks

**Key Decisions**
- Banks with CD ratio below 40% (12 banks including KMB, ANB, PSB, SYN, UCO, UNI, YES, SIB, CBI, IDBI, VIJAYA & FEDERAL) to hold separate meeting to improve performance and meet RBI's 60% benchmark
- State government warned banks with CD ratio below 50% of potential withdrawal of government fund support unless lending improves
- Private sector banks including Bandhan Bank directed to participate in subsidy-based schemes like PMEGP to fulfill social obligations
- Extension requested for Agriculture Debt Relief scheme deadline beyond March 31, 2019

**Performance Highlights**
- CD ratio improved to 52.40% during December quarter with advances growing by ₹1,544 crores, though still below RBI's 60% mandate
- Agriculture Debt Relief scheme covered over 64,000 farmers in 1.5 months; SVAYEM scheme sanctioned around 17,000 loans despite slow disbursements
- Priority sector loans constitute 62% of total advances (well above 40% benchmark); banks achieved 58% of ACP target in FY 2018-19
- 6 districts have CD ratio below 40% requiring attention through DCC/DLRC meetings

**Action Items**
- SLBC and concerned departments to link relevant RBI/government circulars for loan processing timelines
- SLBC, NABARD and banks to adopt common KCC format and make available on SLBC portal
- Banks to focus on districts, blocks and villages with higher agricultural potential for farmer income doubling by 2022

**Key Decisions**
- Under-performing banks required to submit definite action plans to SLBC for improving Credit-Deposit (CD) ratio and priority sector lending
- Banks directed to ensure proper staffing in aspirational district branches to support government scheme implementation
- All banks must separate SHG linkages into three categories (NRLM, Non-NRLM & Mixed) and provide SHG-linked saving account lists to ASRLM
- Banks instructed to resolve insurance premium debit issues for PMJJBY and PMSBY schemes due to insufficient PMJDY account balances

**Performance Highlights**
- Banks achieved 94% of Annual Credit Plan (ACP) target during FY 2018-19
- Priority sector loans reached 61% of total advances with 26% year-over-year growth
- All unbanked villages identified by RBI and DFS successfully covered in the state
- SVAYEM scheme showing poor performance with only 10% of applications receiving loan sanctions and disbursements

**Action Items**
- Banks to focus on backward and forward linkages in agricultural production chain and promote agro-based industries for doubling farmers' income by 2022
- Emphasis on PMAY-PMMY convergence through ground-level instructions to benefit from subsidy elements
- Identified banks (IDBI, KMB, UCO, UNI, ANB, PSB, SYN, YES, SIB, CBI, FED, BOB) to submit roadmaps for CD ratio improvement

**Key Decisions**
• All banks directed to clear pending SVAYEM loan applications by October 15, 2019, with development of an online portal for applications
• Banks with low Credit-Deposit ratios to attend special meetings to develop improvement strategies, with RBI threatening to set individual bank targets if 60% CD ratio isn't achieved
• Shift from district-level to block-level data collection implemented, with SLBC portal data upload deadline set as 15th day of first month of next quarter

**Performance Highlights**
• State CD ratio improved from 52% in March 2019 to 54% in June 2019, but remains well below national benchmark of 60% (47.27% excluding NABARD/NEDFi)
• Agricultural subsidy schemes covered over 220,000 farmers (152,000 under AFCSS, 68,000 under AFIS) with 237,000 additional applications pending
• Only 20% Annual Credit Plan achievement in Q1 FY2019-20: 10% in agriculture, 49% in MSME, 12% in other priority sectors
• SVAYEM scheme severely underperforming with only 17,289 proposals sanctioned from 106,280 sponsored applications

**Action Items**
• Banks to conduct rural surveys and engage with Farmers Producer Organizations, NGOs, and credit-linked clusters to understand local needs
• Individual banks with consistently low performance to be held accountable with specific improvement plans
• Complete coverage of all identified villages under various DFS and RBI concepts through enhanced field visits

**Key Decisions**
- All banks directed to dispose of pending Government Sponsored Scheme loan applications within 30 days
- Banks instructed to eliminate collateral security requirements for MUDRA loans and process PM SVANidhi applications before October 2020 festive season
- SLBC to conduct sub-committee meeting for banks with CD ratio below 20%, with Chief Secretary to engage CEOs of underperforming banks
- Approval granted for India Post Payment Bank to cover 70 villages beyond 5km radius criteria through banking touch-points

**Performance Highlights**
- Assam's Credit Deposit Ratio remains low at around 50%, requiring urgent improvement
- Only 41.91% of savings accounts in Baksa district have digital facilities, with 13.10% current accounts having digital payment modes
- Consistently high pendency in government scheme loan applications raised serious concerns from Chief Secretary

**Action Items**
- All banks to achieve 100% digitization of Baksa district accounts by March 31, 2021, including regular Digital Financial Literacy Camps
- AGVB and Apex Bank to dispose of pending SHG loan applications; LDMs in districts without NABARD DDMs to serve as DLMC Member Secretaries
- Government departments to channel eligible borrowers toward RSETIs to improve credit linkage rates

**Key Decisions**
- Banks with CD ratio below 30% must improve performance or face restrictions on government deposits
- MFIs to be invited as special invitees to future SLBC meetings
- Formation of smaller committees to address issues with schemes like PMEGP and PM SVANidhi
- Higher weightage (125%) assigned to incremental priority sector credit in 14 RBI-identified credit deficit districts

**Performance Highlights**
- PMEGP portfolio shows concerning trend with NPAs reaching Rs.266 crores (51.47% of total portfolio)
- Approximately 70% of ACP achievement in Jun'21 quarter came from MSME sector, indicating need for better agricultural focus
- Private banks like AXIS and YES Bank sanctioned zero Mudra loans during FY 2021-22
- Significant gaps remain in KCC saturation across many districts

**Action Items**
- ASRLM to settle pending training expenses by October 11, 2021
- All private banks directed to actively participate in Mudra loan scheme
- Department of Housing & Urban Affairs and banks to improve coordination for PM SVANidhi implementation
- KVIC to facilitate credit linkages for RSETI-trained candidates and support PMEGP recovery efforts

- All banks directed to lodge Bakijai cases for NPA recovery under PMEGP and government schemes

**Performance Highlights**
- State CD ratio stands at 48.44% as of June 2021, significantly below desired levels
- Priority sector lending at only 25.31% against national benchmark of 40%
- Agriculture advances portfolio critically low at 8% versus benchmark of 18%
- Multiple banks showing zero progress on opening branches in 27 unbanked blocks despite repeated directives

**Action Items**
- All allottee banks to take concrete measures for expeditious opening of branches in unbanked blocks, with government support on infrastructure and security issues
- Banks to focus on financing allied agricultural activities and achieving ACP targets for 2021-22
- State government to support banks in expediting NPA recovery and address infrastructural challenges for new branch openings

summaries.summary:
I notice that the document you've shared appears to contain only page markers ([Page 1], [Page 2], etc.) without any actual content from the SLBC meeting minutes. 

To provide you with a proper summary following your requested format with Key Decisions, Performance Highlights, and Action Items, I would need the actual text content from the Nagaland SLBC meeting minutes from December 2022.

Could you please share the document content again? This would typically include details about:
- Meeting discussions and decisions
- Banking performance data and targets
- Government scheme implementations
- Credit disbursement figures
- Action points for banks and departments

Once you provide the actual meeting content, I'll create a concise summary under 200 words with the specific sections you requested.

**Key Decisions**
- State government directed that Priority Lending Program (PLP) target for FY 2024-25 must be set at 40% of current total advances (Rs 9,970 Cr) and will not approve NABARD's proposed lower target until reviewed with all stakeholders
- State government set specific lending targets: 1,000 education loans, 2,000 skill development loans, and Rs 2,000 Cr for MSME with government providing CGTMSE guarantee fee
- Banks with CD ratio below 40% (IND, PSB, AXIS, BANDHAN, FED, IDBI, IDFC, SIB, YES) were directed to improve performance to reach RBI's 60% benchmark
- Principal Secretary expressed displeasure at banks not sending Regional Heads/Controllers to SLBC meetings despite previous directives

**Performance Highlights**
- CD ratio improved significantly from 52.26% in March 2023 to 61.15% in December 2023, crossing RBI's 60% benchmark for the first time
- Total ACP target achievement reached 89.25% by December 2023
- Priority Sector Lending at Rs 2,107.62 Cr represents only 21.13% of total advances, falling short of RBI's 40% benchmark

**Action Items**
- All banks to expedite opening branches in 22 unbanked blocks and establish brick & mortar branches in villages with 3,000+ population
- Banks to clear pending applications under CMMFI scheme and reduce high NPAs in PMEGP and NULM schemes
- SLBC to create dedicated website for Nagaland and establish secretariat for financial inclusion as per RBI's Lead Bank Scheme

**Key Decisions**
- Finance Commissioner to establish dedicated banking vertical within Finance Department for daily bank interactions and issue resolution
- Chief Minister to convene Credit Summit to launch ACP 2024-25, Modified CMMFI policy guidelines, and Universal Life Insurance Scheme
- Government recruited Credit Managers and Credit Coordinators in each district through SIDBI collaboration
- Banks warned of potential account closure and business withdrawal if they don't participate in CMMFI implementation

**Performance Highlights**
- Credit-Deposit ratio declined from 61.85% to 57.58% in March 2024, below 60% benchmark target
- Overall ACP achievement reached 118.10%, but Agriculture sector performed poorly at only 51.64%
- Priority sector lending at Rs 2,333.54 Cr (22.39% of advances) fell significantly short of 40% benchmark
- High NPA rates in government schemes: PMEGP at 49.25% and NULM at 17.75%

**Action Items**
- All banks to maintain 60% CD ratio throughout FY 2024-25 with quarterly monitoring
- LDMs to conduct Pre-PLP meetings involving line departments, NABARD, RBI and banks
- Banks to establish branches in 22 unbanked blocks and focus expansion beyond four concentrated districts (Dimapur, Kohima, Chumoukedima, Mokokchung)
- Action Taken Reports to be submitted by August 31, 2024

**Key Decisions**
- NABARD announced the Potential Linked Credit Plan for FY 2026-27 totaling Rs 3,088 crore for Nagaland
- Priority sector lending target maintained at 28% of total advances by end of FY 2025-26, as previously approved
- RBI directed banks to implement RSETI opening decisions on priority basis without delays based on lead bank considerations
- State government emphasized need to finalize Annual Credit Plan (ACP) for FY 2026-27 within prescribed timeline

**Performance Highlights**
- CD Ratio improved to 63.64% as of September 2025, up from 59.98% in March 2025, though Kohima district remains low at 32.68%
- Priority sector lending achievement stood at only 39.56% of annual target as of September 2025
- Multiple banks reported nil performance in agriculture and MSME priority sectors, including IDFC, INDUS, YES, ESAF, and SSFB
- Only one RSETI operational in Peren district out of 17 districts statewide

**Action Items**
- LDM Kohima to review CD ratio commitments from 17 bank branches below 40% threshold in next sub-committee meeting
- Concerned banks to increase focus on priority sector advances, particularly in agriculture and MSME segments
- PSB to submit action plan for resolving portal-related issues preventing education loan sanctions

**Key Decisions**
- All banks must achieve ACP (Annual Credit Plan) targets for FY 2021-22 by March 2022
- Banks with CD ratio below 10% (Punjab & Sind Bank, IDFC First Bank, Yes Bank, Federal Bank) must show substantial improvement by March 2022
- SARFAESI Act 2002 implementation in Nagaland effective December 10, 2021; banks to maintain trend analysis and present data at next meeting
- Two new RSETI proposals (Phek and Dimapur) to be examined in sub-committee meeting

**Performance Highlights**
- CD ratio improved from 48.44% (June 2021) to 51.88% (December 2021), but still below RBI benchmark of 60%
- Priority sector advances under ACP achieved only 57% by December 2021
- Several banks (HDFC, IDFC, ICICI, YES Bank, etc.) had NIL outstanding in government sponsored schemes

**Action Items**
- Allottee banks to establish CSP/BCs in all 27 unbanked blocks by March 31, 2022
- Rural Development Department to provide correct longitude/latitude data for 84 villages by March 31, 2022 for DFS DBT-GIS Portal mapping
- SLBC and NRLM to coordinate resolution of pending RSETI training expense reimbursement claims in Peren

**Key Decisions**
- Establishment of 14 functional MSME clusters across Nagaland under two major schemes: 5 clusters under MSE-Common Development Programme (CDP) and 9 clusters under Scheme of Fund for Regeneration of Traditional Industries (SFURTI)
- Focus on food processing sector with 8 clusters dedicated to various food processing activities including honey, spices, pineapple, turmeric, ginger, kiwi, and mushroom processing
- Strategic distribution across 7 districts with Dimapur, Mokokchung, and Zunheboto receiving multiple cluster allocations

**Performance Highlights**
- Comprehensive coverage of traditional industries including bamboo & cane crafts, wooden furniture, and Naga tribal jewellery manufacturing
- Diversified cluster portfolio spanning agriculture, food processing, handicrafts, and animal feed production
- Strong emphasis on value addition in agricultural products and traditional crafts

**Action Items**
- MSME Development & Facilitation Office to coordinate implementation of all 14 clusters as communicated to RBI
- Banks to provide necessary financing support for Common Facility Centers (CFCs) establishment in each cluster
- District-wise monitoring and facilitation of cluster development activities across Dimapur, Mokokchung, Tuensang, Zunheboto, Kiphire, Phek, and Chumoukedima
